content.text: Weaviate is an open-source vector database written in Go.  Unlike Pinecone (cloud-only) or pure libraries like FAISS, Weaviate occupies a middle ground: it can be self-hosted on Docker or Kubernetes, run as a managed cloud service (Weaviate Cloud Services, WCS), or embedded in-process for tests.  Its defining characteristic is a
where every vector is owned by a typed object with named properties -- closer to a document database with built-in embedding than a pure vector store.

Weaviate's architecture has three layers: a core engine (HNSW index + object storage), a pluggable module system (vectorisers, rerankers, generative models), and a query interface (Python v4 client, GraphQL, REST, gRPC).  Key characteristics:
data is organised into Classes (analogous to tables). Each class has typed Properties and a vectoriser module that auto-embeds text at upsert time.
each class gets its own in-memory HNSW index with configurable M and ef parameters.  A flat index option exists for small collections.
text2vec-openai, text2vec-huggingface, text2vec-cohere, and others auto-embed objects at import time without a separate embedding step.
combines BM25 sparse retrieval with dense ANN search in a single query, controlled by an alpha parameter.
the generative-openai module enables RAG directly in the query layer: retrieve objects and pass them to an LLM in one API call.
first-class support for per-tenant isolated shards within a single class, useful for SaaS applications.

attrs.alt: Weaviate architecture showing three layers: core engine with schema (Article/Author classes), HNSW index, and BM25 object store; vectoriser modules (text2vec-openai, huggingface, cohere, generative-openai); and query interface (nearText, nearVector, hybrid, where filter, generative); plus four deployment options (Docker, Kubernetes, WCS, Embedded)

content.text:
# Python client v4
pip install weaviate-client

# For local Docker deployment:
docker run -d \
  -p 8080:8080 \
  -p 50051:50051 \
  -e AUTHENTICATION_ANONYMOUS_ACCESS_ENABLED=true \
  cr.weaviate.io/semitechnologies/weaviate:latest

import weaviate
import os

# Connect to local Weaviate (Docker)
client = weaviate.connect_to_local()

# Connect to Weaviate Cloud Services
client = weaviate.connect_to_wcs(
    cluster_url=os.environ['WCS_URL'],
    auth_credentials=weaviate.auth.AuthApiKey(os.environ['WCS_API_KEY']),
    headers={'X-OpenAI-Api-Key': os.environ['OPENAI_API_KEY']},  # for text2vec-openai
)

# Embedded Weaviate (in-process, no Docker needed -- dev/test only)
client = weaviate.connect_to_embedded(
    version='1.24.0',
    environment_variables={
        'ENABLE_MODULES': 'text2vec-openai,generative-openai'
    }
)

print(client.is_ready())  # True
client.close()  # always close the connection when done
is a named collection of objects with a fixed set of typed
.  You declare which vectoriser module to use at the class level; Weaviate then automatically embeds the relevant text properties on every upsert.

import weaviate
import weaviate.classes as wvc

client = weaviate.connect_to_local()

# Create a class with text2vec-openai vectoriser
client.collections.create(
    name='Article',
    vectorizer_config=wvc.config.Configure.Vectorizer.text2vec_openai(
        model='text-embedding-3-small',
    ),
    generative_config=wvc.config.Configure.Generative.openai(
        model='gpt-4o-mini',
    ),
    properties=[
        wvc.config.Property(name='title',    data_type=wvc.config.DataType.TEXT),
        wvc.config.Property(name='body',     data_type=wvc.config.DataType.TEXT),
        wvc.config.Property(name='source',   data_type=wvc.config.DataType.TEXT,
                            skip_vectorization=True),  # not embedded
        wvc.config.Property(name='pub_date', data_type=wvc.config.DataType.DATE),
        wvc.config.Property(name='views',    data_type=wvc.config.DataType.INT),
    ],
    vector_index_config=wvc.config.Configure.VectorIndex.hnsw(
        distance_metric=wvc.config.VectorDistances.COSINE,
        ef=128,
        m=64,
        ef_construction=256,
    ),
)

print('Article collection created')
The vectoriser embeds the concatenation of all text properties that are
.  Non-text properties (dates, integers, booleans) are always excluded from the embedding but remain filterable.

Mark metadata-only properties like IDs, categories, or dates with skip_vectorization=True.  Including them in the embedding dilutes the semantic signal from the text and can reduce recall for natural-language queries.
Weaviate v4 client uses a collection-based API.  The vectoriser auto-embeds text at upsert time; you do not compute embeddings yourself unless you use

import weaviate
import weaviate.classes as wvc
from datetime import datetime, timezone

client = weaviate.connect_to_local()
articles = client.collections.get('Article')

# Insert a single object -- vectoriser auto-embeds title + body
uuid = articles.data.insert({
    'title': 'How HNSW works',
    'body': 'HNSW is a graph-based ANN algorithm for approximate nearest neighbour search.',
    'source': 'ml-blog',
    'pub_date': datetime(2024, 1, 15, tzinfo=timezone.utc).isoformat(),
    'views': 4200,
})
print(uuid)  # auto-generated UUIDv4

# Batch insert (recommended for > 10 objects)
documents = [
    {'title': 'Product Quantization', 'body': 'PQ compresses vectors into compact codes.',
     'source': 'ml-blog', 'pub_date': '2024-02-01T00:00:00Z', 'views': 3100},
    {'title': 'IVF Index', 'body': 'IVF clusters vectors into Voronoi cells.',
     'source': 'ml-blog', 'pub_date': '2024-03-10T00:00:00Z', 'views': 2800},
]

with articles.batch.dynamic() as batch:
    for doc in documents:
        batch.add_object(properties=doc)

# Check for failed objects in the batch
if articles.batch.failed_objects:
    print('Failed:', articles.batch.failed_objects)

client.close()
Batch dynamic mode auto-tunes the batch size and concurrency based on the server response times.  For very large imports (millions of objects), use

is Weaviate's highest-level query: you pass a natural language string, the configured vectoriser module embeds it server-side, and the HNSW index returns the closest objects.  No client-side embedding is required.

import weaviate
import weaviate.classes as wvc

client = weaviate.connect_to_local()
articles = client.collections.get('Article')

results = articles.query.near_text(
    query='approximate nearest neighbour search',
    limit=5,
    return_metadata=wvc.query.MetadataQuery(score=True, distance=True),
    return_properties=['title', 'body', 'source'],
    filters=wvc.query.Filter.by_property('views').greater_than(1000),
)

for obj in results.objects:
    print(f"dist={obj.metadata.distance:.4f}  {obj.properties['title']}")
# dist=0.1821  How HNSW works
# dist=0.2104  Product Quantization

client.close()
returned is the cosine distance (1 - cosine_similarity) when the index uses cosine metric.  Lower distance means higher semantic similarity.  The equivalent
Weaviate's hybrid search fuses BM25 sparse retrieval with dense ANN similarity using Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) or a linear score blend.  The
At alpha=1.0, the result is pure vector search; at alpha=0.0, pure BM25.  Values around 0.5-0.75 typically outperform either alone.

results = articles.query.hybrid(
    query='HNSW graph traversal algorithm',  # used for both BM25 and vector search
    alpha=0.75,                              # 75% vector, 25% BM25
    limit=10,
    return_metadata=wvc.query.MetadataQuery(score=True),
    return_properties=['title', 'body'],
)

for obj in results.objects:
    print(f"score={obj.metadata.score:.4f}  {obj.properties['title']}")
Hybrid search is automatically available for any class that has a text vectoriser; BM25 indexes are built on all text properties that are not marked

Weaviate supports property-level filters on all queries.  Filters are applied as a pre-filter before ANN search on the HNSW index -- this is more precise than post-filtering but can degrade recall when the filter is very selective.

import weaviate.classes as wvc
from datetime import datetime, timezone

# Simple equality filter
f1 = wvc.query.Filter.by_property('source').equal('ml-blog')

# Numeric range filter
f2 = wvc.query.Filter.by_property('views').greater_than(2000)

# Date filter
cutoff = datetime(2024, 2, 1, tzinfo=timezone.utc)
f3 = wvc.query.Filter.by_property('pub_date').greater_than(cutoff)

# Combining filters with AND / OR
combined = f1 & f2 & f3            # AND: all conditions must match
either   = f1 | f2                 # OR:  any condition matches

results = articles.query.near_text(
    query='vector compression',
    limit=5,
    filters=combined,
)
Weaviate's generative module passes retrieved objects directly to an LLM within the same query, eliminating the need to retrieve then call the LLM separately.  Two modes are supported:
generates one LLM response per retrieved object, using a prompt template with placeholders for object properties.
generates one LLM response for the entire retrieved set, passing all objects as a batch.  This is the standard RAG pattern.

import weaviate
import weaviate.classes as wvc

client = weaviate.connect_to_wcs(
    cluster_url='https://your-cluster.weaviate.network',
    auth_credentials=weaviate.auth.AuthApiKey('YOUR_WCS_KEY'),
    headers={'X-OpenAI-Api-Key': 'YOUR_OPENAI_KEY'},
)
articles = client.collections.get('Article')

# Grouped task: retrieve top-5, then generate one answer for all
response = articles.generate.near_text(
    query='How do vector databases handle similarity search?',
    limit=5,
    grouped_task=(
        'Using only the provided articles, explain how vector databases handle '
        'similarity search. Be concise and cite the article titles.'
    ),
    return_properties=['title', 'body'],
)

print(response.generated)  # LLM answer
print('Based on articles:')
for obj in response.objects:
    print(' -', obj.properties['title'])

# Single prompt: one response per object
response = articles.generate.near_text(
    query='ANN algorithms',
    limit=3,
    single_prompt='Summarise this article in one sentence: {body}',
)
for obj in response.objects:
    print(obj.generated)  # per-object LLM output

client.close()
(or equivalent) module to be enabled on the Weaviate instance.  The LLM call happens server-side: only the query string and the final LLM response are transferred to the client.
Weaviate supports per-tenant shards within a class.  Each tenant gets its own isolated HNSW index and object storage partition, preventing data leakage between tenants.

import weaviate
import weaviate.classes as wvc

client = weaviate.connect_to_local()

# Create a multi-tenant collection
client.collections.create(
    name='TenantArticle',
    multi_tenancy_config=wvc.config.Configure.multi_tenancy(enabled=True),
    vectorizer_config=wvc.config.Configure.Vectorizer.text2vec_openai(),
    properties=[
        wvc.config.Property(name='title', data_type=wvc.config.DataType.TEXT),
        wvc.config.Property(name='body',  data_type=wvc.config.DataType.TEXT),
    ],
)
coll = client.collections.get('TenantArticle')

# Create tenants explicitly
coll.tenants.create([wvc.tenants.Tenant(name='org-acme'),
                     wvc.tenants.Tenant(name='org-globex')])

# Upsert into a specific tenant
coll.with_tenant('org-acme').data.insert({'title': 'ACME Guide', 'body': 'ACME data...'})

# Query is automatically scoped to the tenant
results = coll.with_tenant('org-acme').query.near_text(
    query='guides', limit=5
)
client.close()
Flat: id + float vector + metadata dict
Yes -- vectoriser modules at upsert time
No -- client must embed before upsert
BM25 + vector, RRF or linear blend
No -- client must call LLM separately
Yes -- per-tenant shards in one class
Self-hosted / full control, schema-rich data, built-in RAG
Fastest managed setup, minimal ops overhead
Including metadata properties in the embedding.
Fields like 'source', 'author', or 'category' dilute semantic meaning.  Mark them skip_vectorization=True so they are filterable but not embedded.
The v4 client holds a gRPC connection.  Always call client.close() or use 'with weaviate.connect_to_local() as client:' context management.
Using nearText without enabling a vectoriser module.
If the Weaviate instance does not have text2vec-openai enabled, nearText raises a module-not-found error.  Use nearVector with your own embeddings instead.
Network errors and schema mismatches fail silently unless you inspect batch.failed_objects after each batch.
Weaviate defaults ef=64 and M=64.  Lower ef dramatically reduces recall; raise it to 128 or 256 if recall matters more than query latency.
Chunk documents to 200-400 tokens before storing.  Long objects produce diluted embeddings.
Use batch.dynamic() for imports larger than 20 objects; it auto-tunes batch size and concurrency.
Store the original text in a property so retrieved objects are self-contained for RAG context assembly.
Use hybrid search (alpha=0.5-0.75) by default for user-facing search.  Fall back to pure nearText only after benchmarking shows no BM25 improvement.
For production self-hosting, set ef=128 and M=64 at class creation.  These cannot be changed after import without rebuilding the index.
Enable multi-tenancy at class creation time.  It cannot be added to an existing class.
Run Weaviate with at least 4 GB RAM per 1M 768-dim vectors to keep the HNSW graph in memory.
Weaviate is a schema-driven, open-source vector database with HNSW at its core.  It can be self-hosted, run on WCS, or embedded in-process.
Classes define the schema; vectoriser modules auto-embed text properties at upsert time.  You do not compute embeddings separately when using a vectoriser.
nearText uses the server-side vectoriser; nearVector accepts raw float embeddings from any source.  Both search the same HNSW index.
Hybrid search fuses BM25 and ANN results with an alpha blend.  Values around 0.5-0.75 typically outperform pure vector search.
The generative module enables RAG in a single query call: retrieve objects and prompt an LLM without a separate round-trip.
Multi-tenancy creates per-tenant HNSW shards within a class, providing data isolation without separate collections.
